content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 31, 1993


Honorable Paula L. Boland
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, California  94249-0001

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-93-036

Dear Assemblywoman Boland:

     This is in reply to your request for advice concerning the
permissible use of campaign funds under the Political Reform Act
(the "Act")1.

QUESTION

     May you use campaign funds to supplement the Assembly income
received by a member of your Assembly office staff?

CONCLUSION

     The Act permits you to use campaign funds to supplement the
Assembly income of a member of your Assembly staff only when the
campaign funds used to compensate the staff member are: (a) for
services that are not already being compensated with the staff
member's Assembly pay, (b) the services are directly related to a
legislative or governmental purpose, and (c) the amount in
campaign funds paid to the staff member is reasonable compensation
for the services rendered. However, the Legislative Code of
Ethics (Government Code Section 8920, et seq.), which is not
within our jurisdiction, may nonetheless prohibit you from doing
this. Please consult the Legislative Counsel or the Joint
Committee on Legislative Ethics in this regard.


___________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.



428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-036
Page 2


ANALYSIS

Section 89513(b) states that campaign funds may be used to
pay for the cost of professional services if the services are
directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental
purpose.

Implicit in this provision is the requirement that the amount
in campaign funds that is paid for professional services is not
far in excess of the reasonable cost for similar professional
services in similar circumstances. Such a payment would not be
permitted because it would take on the character of a gift that is
totally unrelated to a political, legislative or governmental
purpose.

Applying this analysis to your question, when a staff member
receives compensation from the Assembly to perform specific
duties, the amount of that compensation is deemed to be a
reasonable amount. Consequently, campaign funds cannot be paid to
the staff member to supplement the Assembly compensation already
paid for performance of those duties.

However, campaign funds may be used to pay the staff member
for performing legislative or governmental duties that are in
addition to those for which he or she is already compensated by
the Assembly if the following conditions are met. First, the
duties must be directly related to a legislative or governmental
purpose. Second, the amount in campaign funds paid to the staff
member must not exceed an amount that is reasonable in exchange
for the services rendered.

We caution you, however, that our advice only interprets the
provisions of the Political Reform Act because that is the extent
of our jurisdiction. It is possible that other laws, outside of
the Act, prohibit you from making this use of campaign funds.
Specifically, we refer you to the Legislative Code of Ethics,
located in Government Code Section 8920, et seq. You should
consult with the Legislative Counsel or the Joint Committee on
Legislative Ethics on these and any other provisions outside the
Act that could apply.

Our File No. A-93-036
Page 3


Should you have questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Scott Hallabrin
Senior Staff Counsel
